hook: UCL's overstuffed Egyptology study collection, all dust and labelled drawers
facts: Built on the collection of pioneering archaeologist Flinders Petrie, sold to UCL in 1913
Holds roughly 80,000 objects, one of the largest Egyptian and Sudanese collections anywhere
Tucked at the end of Malet Place behind the science library, easy to miss
Includes the world's oldest dress, the Tarkhan linen, dated to around 3000 BC
w: Flinders Petrie dug pottery shards and trinkets nobody else wanted, then built a science of dating from them. His 80,000 objects fill cramped cases down a back lane at UCL, lit by hand torches some evenings. Among them, a linen dress older than the pyramids. Egyptology without the crowds.

hook: British satire and comic art, Gillray to the Beano, on a quiet Fitzrovia street
facts: Founded by the Cartoon Art Trust, opened to the public in 2006
Moved to 63 Wells Street, north of Oxford Street, in 2019
Holds original artwork spanning Hogarth and Gillray to Steve Bell and Posy Simmonds
Runs a research library of thousands of comics and books upstairs
w: Three centuries of British rudeness under one roof: Gillray's bloated royals, Bell's lizard politicians, the inked originals of the Beano and Dennis the Menace. A small charity keeps the lights on north of Oxford Street while the crowds shuffle past below. Proof the nation has always preferred mockery to monuments.

hook: Britain's first children's charity, and the heartbreak of the leaving tokens
facts: Tells the story of the Foundling Hospital, opened by Thomas Coram in 1739
Mothers left a small token, a coin or scrap of fabric, to identify a child they hoped to reclaim
Hogarth and Handel were early patrons, making it Britain's first public art gallery
The building holds Rococo interiors saved from the demolished original hospital
w: Desperate mothers handed over babies and left a token, a bent coin, a button, a snipped heart of cloth, so they might one day prove which child was theirs. Most never came back. The cases of tokens here gut you quietly. Coram's hospital also gave Britain its first art gallery, courtesy of Hogarth and Handel.

hook: Franklin's only surviving home, with 1,200 human bones under the floor
facts: 36 Craven Street housed Benjamin Franklin from 1757 to 1775
In 1998 over 1,200 pieces of human and animal bone were dug from the basement
The bones came from an anatomy school run by Franklin's landlady's son-in-law, William Hewson
Now the world's only surviving Franklin residence, run as a museum
w: Franklin lodged here for eighteen years, flying kites and plotting revolution a stroll from Trafalgar Square. In 1998 builders found 1,200 human bones beneath the garden room. Not murder, but a quiet anatomy school run by the landlady's son-in-law, dissecting bodies snatched for science. Trepanned skulls still on show.

hook: The thespians' parish, where Punch and Judy and Pygmalion both began
facts: Designed by Inigo Jones in the 1630s, the first wholly classical church in England
Pepys recorded the first English Punch and Judy show under its portico in 1662
Walls lined with memorials to actors from Boris Karloff to Charlie Chaplin
The portico opens Shaw's Pygmalion, later My Fair Lady
w: Inigo Jones reputedly built England's plainest church facing the piazza, then locals demanded the altar end face it, so the grand portico became a back door. Pepys watched the first English Punch and Judy beneath it in 1662. Inside, plaques to Karloff, Chaplin, Vivien Leigh. Pygmalion opens on this very step.

hook: A lone bronze camel and rider, easy to walk straight past
facts: Sculpted by Major Cecil Brown, who served with the Corps, and unveiled in 1921
Stands in Victoria Embankment Gardens east of Charing Cross
Commemorates the 346 men of the Imperial Camel Corps killed in the First World War
The Corps fought across Egypt, Sinai and Palestine, supporting the desert campaign
w: Half life-size and tucked among the Embankment shrubbery, a bronze trooper rides his camel through a London drizzle that would have baffled him. Cut by a major who served alongside the men he carved, it names 346 dead of a forgotten desert war. The unlikeliest cavalry, stranded by the Thames.

hook: A creaking back-alley boozer once nicknamed the Bucket of Blood
facts: A pub has stood on this Rose Street site since at least the early 18th century
Dryden was beaten by hired thugs in adjoining Rose Alley in 1679, the Rose Alley Ambuscade
Once hosted bare-knuckle prizefights, earning the nickname Bucket of Blood
Grade II listed, reached down a narrow covered passage off Garrick Street
legend: They say the poet Dryden was ambushed in the alley on the orders of a slighted earl
w: Down a passage barely wide enough for shoulders sits a low timber pub they once called the Bucket of Blood for the bare-knuckle bouts upstairs. In 1679 the poet Dryden was set upon in the alley outside, legend says by thugs hired by a furious earl. Order a pint, mind the beams, and watch the door.

hook: Where the Brownings married in secret, above bricked-up catacombs
facts: Thomas Hardwick's church was completed in 1817 on Marylebone Road
Robert Browning and Elizabeth Barrett married here in secret in 1846, then eloped to Italy
The crypt held around 850 coffins until they were reinterred at Brookwood in the 1980s
The emptied vaults now house a healing and counselling centre
w: In 1846 Elizabeth Barrett slipped from her father's house, married Robert Browning here without a word to anyone, and fled to Florence. Their certificate survives in the archive. Beneath the floor, catacombs once packed with 850 coffins, since emptied to Brookwood and turned over to a counselling centre. Romance over a crypt.

hook: Britain's first public gallery devoted wholly to photography, in a Soho warehouse
facts: Founded in 1971, the UK's first independent gallery dedicated to photography
Moved to a converted textile warehouse on Ramillies Street in 2012
Awards the annual Deutsche Borse Photography Foundation Prize
Tucked on a narrow side street between Oxford Street and Soho proper
w: When it opened in 1971 no public gallery in Britain took photography seriously as art; this one staked the claim and won. Now stacked inside a black-clad former rag-trade warehouse on a Soho backstreet, five floors of prints, a camera obscura window, and the prize that crowns the year. The medium's London home.

hook: A jewel-box Byzantine chapel, all that survives of a vanished hospital
facts: Designed by John Loughborough Pearson, begun 1891 for the Middlesex Hospital
Its gold mosaic interior was completed in 1929, decades after the architect's death
The hospital around it was demolished in 2008, the chapel kept and restored
Now stands alone in Pearson Square amid the Fitzroy Place development
w: Generations of patients prayed here before surgery in the old Middlesex Hospital. When the wrecking ball came in 2008, everything fell but this: a tiny Byzantine box glittering with gold mosaic and seventeen kinds of marble, never quite finished. It survives, marooned in a glass-and-steel square, a lit ghost of the ward.

hook: An Italianate terrace garden sitting on the roof of an electricity substation
facts: Built 1905 to a Baroque design by Charles Stanley Peach off Duke Street
Raised on the roof of a working electricity substation, with domed pavilions at each end
The Duke of Westminster demanded a garden to replace the communal ground lost below
Reopened to the public in 2013 after long closure, now with a cafe
w: Lose the Mayfair garden to a power station and the Duke of Westminster's answer was simple: put the garden on the roof. So a balustraded Italian terrace with stone pavilions sits atop a humming substation, transformers below, planters above. Climb the steps off Duke Street for a quiet bench over the grid.

hook: Wren's favourite church, where Blake was christened and a courtyard market hums
facts: Consecrated 1684, the one Wren church built on a fresh site of his own choosing
William Blake was baptised at its Grinling Gibbons font in 1757
Gibbons also carved the limewood reredos, among his finest work
A daily market fills the courtyard, antiques to crafts, funding the church's upkeep
w: Wren said this one pleased him most, the rare church he built from scratch on open ground. At the Grinling Gibbons font, carved with birds and fruit, the infant William Blake was christened in 1757. Outside, a scruffy courtyard market trades antiques and crafts to keep the roof on. Visionaries and bric-a-brac.

hook: A Manchester Square mansion crammed with one of Europe's great armouries
facts: Housed in Hertford House, bequeathed to the nation in 1897 and free to enter
Holds arms and armour from the 10th to the 19th centuries across three galleries
Much was bought in 1871 from the de Nieuwerkerke and Meyrick collections
Includes wheel-lock guns made for Louis XIII, Louis XIV and Tsar Nicholas I
w: Most come for the Fragonards and the Laughing Cavalier, then stumble into the basement and the gleaming racks of steel. Three galleries of European armour and an Eastern hoard, etched breastplates and royal wheel-lock guns, gathered by a French-raised heir and left to the nation. Free, and almost always empty downstairs.

hook: A cobbled Georgian cut-through with a sinister cinematic afterlife
facts: A narrow covered passage dating from 1746, linking Rathbone Street to Newman Street
The Newman Arms pub at its mouth was built around 1730
Opens Michael Powell's 1960 thriller Peeping Tom, filmed on these stones
George Orwell is said to have drunk at the Newman Arms when living nearby
legend: They say the passage's gloom long drew streetwalkers and gives it a Ripper-era chill after dark
w: Duck through the low arch beside the Newman Arms and the city drops away into damp cobbles and brick. Powell opened Peeping Tom here in 1960, a killer stalking the dark, and the film was so reviled it ended his career. Orwell supposedly drank at the pub. After nightfall the passage still earns its shivers.
